title: Passion fruit soft caramels
subtitle: Butter caramels with a sharp passion fruit finish
excerpt: Soft, chewy butter caramels with a bright edge of passion fruit pulp added off the heat. Poured into a lined tin, set overnight, then cut and wrapped in waxed paper squares.
sourceNotes: Soft butter caramels are documented in French and British confectionery from the nineteenth century. The addition of fruit purées and acidic flavourings to a butter caramel base, balancing the sweetness with sharpness, is a standard technique in French confectionery. Passion fruit pairs with caramel because its high acidity and floral flavour survive the heat without cooking out entirely if added off the heat. The method here follows the classic butter caramel approach with a passion fruit finish.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Set overnight at room temperature before cutting. Wrapped caramels keep for 2 weeks at room temperature.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: No baking required. Cooked on the hob to 124°C (firm-ball stage).

glossaryTerms.definition: A point in sugar cooking at approximately 122 to 126°C. A small drop in cold water can be formed into a firm ball that holds its shape but is still slightly pliable. This is the target for soft, chewy caramels: below this temperature the caramel will be too soft; above 130°C it will become too hard and brittle when cool.

Volatile and acidic ingredients like passion fruit pulp, citrus juice, or spirits are added to a cooked caramel after removing it from the heat. Adding them to a boiling caramel would cause violent spattering (the water content turns to steam instantly) and the heat would drive off most of the flavour compounds. Off the heat, the flavour is preserved and the splattering is minimised.

content.text: Sugar reaches 124°C in this recipe. If it contacts skin, run cold water over the area for at least ten minutes.
These caramels are cooked to the
for a chewy but not hard texture. The passion fruit is added
to preserve its flavour and avoid violent spattering. A sugar thermometer is essential.

attrs.prepNote: strained of seeds, from about 4 passion fruits

content.text: Grease and line a 20 cm square tin. Combine the sugar, cream, butter, glucose syrup, and salt in a deep, heavy saucepan. Stir over medium heat until the sugar dissolves. Bring to a boil and cook without stirring, swirling the pan occasionally, until the mixture reaches 124°C on a sugar thermometer. This takes about 15 minutes. The caramel will be a deep amber colour.
Add the passion fruit and set

Remove from heat. Wait 30 seconds for the boiling to subside, then pour in the passion fruit pulp carefully — it will bubble and spit. Stir well with a heatproof spatula until the passion fruit is fully incorporated. Pour immediately into the prepared tin. Allow to cool at room temperature overnight. Cut into small rectangles with a lightly oiled knife and wrap individually in squares of waxed paper, twisting the ends.
